The media published information about secret meetings between UNRWA and‚Å§ the leaders of ‚Å¢Palestinian terrorist groups.
The former commissioner ‚Å£general of ‚Äåthe agency, Pierre Krahenb√ºhl, held talks in Beirut in February ‚Å£2017 with‚Äã representatives‚Å£ of the terrorist organizations Hamas, Islamic Jihad (PIJ), the Popular Front for the Liberation ‚Å¢of‚Å£ Palestine (PFLP) and ‚Äçothers. The meeting was also attended by‚Å£ the head of‚Å¢ UNRWA in Lebanon, ‚ÄåHakam Shawan, as‚Å§ well as prominent leaders of terrorist organizations, including Ali Baraka and Abu Imad al-Rifai.
According to the publication, Krahenbuhl sought to establish cooperation with these groups, saying‚Å¢ that the agency and the militants must work as a ‚Äúunited front.‚Äù He urged meeting participants‚Å§ to refrain from publicly criticizing ‚ÄçUNRWA to avoid ‚Äçbacklash from donor ‚Äåcountries that could lead ‚Å£to cuts in ‚Å£funding ‚Äçfor ‚Äåthe agency.
Hamas terrorist leader Ali Barak, who handled the ‚Äåorganization‚Äôs foreign relations with Iran and Syria and later came‚Å¢ under US sanctions, was one of the meeting participants. Abu‚Å§ Imad al-Rifai, ‚Å¢who represents Islamic Jihad in Lebanon, has previously said his group sent suicide‚Äã bombers to attack Western military personnel. The presence of such figures at the negotiations with UNRWA caused a‚Äç wide resonance.
Documents‚Å¢ show that Krahenb√ºhl openly discussed the possibility of changing or overturning UNRWA decisions at the request‚Å§ of terrorist‚Äç organizations. He emphasized the importance of a ‚Äúspirit of‚Å§ partnership‚Äù and ‚Å£invited representatives of Hamas and ‚Å§other groups‚Äç to private discussions on controversial issues.
UN Watch director Hillel Neuer noted‚Å£ that such contacts between UNRWA and Hamas were not ‚Äçisolated. In particular, the agency‚Äôs regional directors not only met with militant leaders, but also congratulated them on their anniversaries of activity, despite the international recognition of Hamas as a terrorist organization.
This information became especially resonant after the events of October ‚Äç7, when Hamas militants‚Äç committed massacres.
It‚Å¢ is reported that some UNRWA personnel took part in Hamas activities by collaborating with al-Qassam. In addition, UNRWA-run schools were used ‚Å¢to promote anti-Semitism and violence. One of the agency‚Äôs teachers was even seen organizing ‚Å§anti-Israeli protests in the Netherlands on‚Å£ behalf ‚Å§of a group associated‚Å£ with Hamas terrorists.
Earlier, Kursor wrote that the pogrom in Amsterdam could‚Äå have been organized by an UNRWA teacher.‚Äç

How can UNRWA restore its credibility after allegations‚Äç of compromised neutrality in middle eastern politics? Title: A Troubling Alliance: UNRWA and Palestinian Groups
Interviewer (Time.news Editor): Good morning, and thank you for‚Äå joining us today. We have with us Dr. Sarah‚Äã Lewis, ‚Å¢an expert in Middle Eastern politics and humanitarian initiatives. Dr. Lewis, there‚Äôs been some alarming news regarding UNRWA‚Äôs past‚Å¢ dealings with Palestinian militant groups. Can you help us unpack‚Å¢ the implications of these ‚Äçsecret meetings?
Dr. Sarah Lewis: Good morning, and thank you for having me. Indeed, the revelations surrounding‚Å£ the meetings between former UNRWA commissioner Pierre Krahenb√ºhl and ‚Å§leaders from groups like Hamas and Islamic Jihad are quite concerning. These meetings‚Äå raise‚Äã significant questions about the integrity and neutrality of humanitarian organizations ‚Äåin conflict zones.
Interviewer: Absolutely. The report indicates that Krahenb√ºhl sought to establish a ‚Äúunited front‚Äù with ‚Å£these groups. What do you think his motivations were?
Dr. Lewis: Krahenb√ºhl likely believed that establishing cooperation could facilitate aid delivery ‚Å£and improve conditions for those affected by conflict. However, ‚Äãthis approach ‚Å¢can dangerously ‚Å§blur the lines between humanitarian action and political agendas. Dr. Lewis: Yes, he did. By aligning with such‚Å§ groups,‚Äç there‚Äôs an inherent risk of the agency being perceived as ‚Äåcomplicit in their‚Äã actions, which could provoke a backlash‚Äå from donor nations. Such backlash could lead to funding cuts, ultimately harming the very communities that UNRWA aims to support.
Interviewer: You mentioned the potential consequences for donor relations. How significant ‚Äçare these‚Äç funding streams for UNRWA, and what could this mean for their‚Å¢ operations on ‚Äçthe ground?
Dr. Lewis: Funding is critically important for UNRWA, as‚Å¢ it relies heavily‚Äç on international ‚Äçdonations to provide‚Å§ essential services like education, healthcare, and emergency assistance. A reduction‚Äç in funds could lead to ‚Å¢cutbacks in these services, exacerbating the humanitarian‚Äå crisis already present in Palestinian ‚Å£territories. It‚Å¢ could ‚Å§also‚Å§ lead to increased instability ‚Å§in ‚Äçan‚Å¢ already volatile region.

Dr. ‚Å¢Lewis: The‚Äå timing ‚Å¢of these revelations could be linked ‚Å¢to ‚Å£growing scrutiny of humanitarian organizations and their relationships with militant groups. With the ongoing ‚Äãconflict ‚Äåand shifting‚Äå political landscapes in the Middle East, stakeholders are increasingly being held accountable for their‚Å¢ affiliations and actions. The media plays a crucial role in uncovering these ‚Å¢relationships and prompting‚Äç necessary‚Äç discourse.
Interviewer: What steps do you think UNRWA‚Äå should take moving forward to restore trust and maintain its operations effectively?
Dr. ‚Å£Lewis: First and foremost, transparency is key. UNRWA should conduct a thorough review of‚Äã its policies and engage independent audits to ‚Äãreassure stakeholders that‚Å£ they operate ‚Äçwith integrity. They must reaffirm their commitment to neutrality and work diligently‚Äå to rebuild relationships with donor countries while ensuring that aid reaches those in need without political ‚Äãentanglements.

Dr. Lewis: Thank you for having me. It‚Äôs crucial that we continue to discuss ‚Äãthese issues comprehensively to ensure that humanitarian efforts‚Å§ remain ‚Å§focused‚Äå on those who need‚Å§ them‚Äå most.
